data.title: Vertex: Expensive, Losing Customers, And Shedding Margins (Downgrade)
data.description:
With the stock market dancing around nervously near all-time highs, I continue to encourage investors to become very selective stock-pickers. In my view, the stock market is bound to catch up with a recessionary economy, and pull down shares of richly valued stocks with little fundamental backing.

Vertex, Inc. (NASDAQ:VERX), in my view, is one of those names. The tax compliance software company is already down ~30% since the start of the year: and yet, the stock still hasn't shaken off the majority of its valuation premium that continues to become more unjustifiable as the company reports decelerating growth and waning margins.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Vertex in March, when the stock was trading near $35 per share. Today, Vertex sits about ~10% higher, even though many of the company's core fundamental metrics have continued to decline. With this in mind, I'm downgrading Vertex to a sell rating.

To me, these are the core red flags that Vertex faces:

  • Customer churn reflects a tough sales environment: Over the past year, Vertex's new customer acquisition counts have begun to slow, finally decaying to a net customer decline in its most recent quarter. While the company notes that this churn is primarily coming from smaller customers, the fact remains that the company's new business momentum is insufficient to offset net churn.
  • Headwinds as cross-border trade slows down: One of Vertex's core competencies is sales and VAT tax compliance, which becomes even more complex for multinational companies dealing with customs compliance and other headaches of doing business overseas. Tariffs have slowed both inbound and outbound shipments from the U.S., which is another potential headwind to Vertex's mandate to help companies with their tax compliance.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margins are peeling back: Despite slowing top-line growth, Vertex has not taken the same playbook as many of its tech peers, and it is ramping up spending, particularly in what I view to be non-productive G&A (corporate overhead). As a result, adjusted EBITDA margins are falling behind.

Despite these weaknesses, Vertex remains quite expensively valued for a mid-teens growth company. At current share prices near $38, Vertex trades at a market cap of $5.95 billion. After we net off the $270.4 million of cash and $335.8 million of debt on Vertex's latest balance sheet (yet another reason to be suspicious of this stock: it holds a net debt position, whereas most software peers have net cash), the company's resulting enterprise value is $6.02 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Vertex has maintained its full-year growth expectations at $760-$780 million in revenue (14-15% y/y growth), while also keeping its adjusted EBITDA range (the midpoint of which implies a 21% margin) unchanged.

Vertex's valuation sits at:

  • 7.9x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 37x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

We note that other large-cap enterprise software companies whose growth rates have peeled back to the mid-teens, such as Workday (WDAY), now command valuation multiples in a ~6-7x range. In my view, Vertex is a terrible deal - not only is the stock expensive, but fundamentally it's showing weakness across both the top and bottom line. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q1 Download

Let's now go through Vertex's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Vertex's revenue grew 13% y/y to $177.1 million, basically only in-line with Wall Street's expectations of $177.0 million. Revenue growth also decelerated from a 15% y/y pace in Q4. This, plus the fact that even Vertex's maintained guidance at 14-15% y/y growth requires acceleration in the back half of FY25 (which is tough to bank on in a tight macroeconomy) is the core reason why shares of Vertex wobbled after the earnings print in early May, though the stock has since recovered all of those losses (hence my skepticism on this investment).

To me, one of the core negative indicators here is the company's slowdown in customer count. Already in Q4, the company's net-new customer additions had slowed down to fewer than 100 customers. In Q1, the company actually lost 10 customers sequentially versus Q4.

Management dismissed these customer losses as smaller customers that aren't as material to the top line, while Vertex's sales teams are working on landing higher-value accounts. David DeStefano, the company's CEO, noted on the Q1 earnings call that the company's sales pipeline remains robust, and that tailwinds, including mergers, are increasing in complexity and driving adoption of Vertex solutions. Per his remarks on the earnings call:

Another persistent tailwind is business change resulting from mergers and acquisitions expanding into new geographies or opening up new business channels like omnichannel sales. Anytime there is change, there's added complexity for our customers to manage. As everyone has seen the volatility in the macroeconomic environment is unprecedented. However, I am pleased with our first quarter results and our market opportunity. We have not seen elongated sales cycles or deal slippage at this point. Our pipeline continues to grow and that is something we will continue to monitor carefully as the year develops and companies and regulators adapt to the changing environment.

Still, we also find Vertex's slowing growth amid increasing spend to be quite disappointing. The chart below shows that sales and marketing expenses (on a pro forma basis, excluding primarily stock comp) rose 17% y/y to $41.8 million, faster than revenue. General and administrative spending, which I view to be the least productive component of OpEx, grew 33% y/y to $36.6 million:

As a result, adjusted EBITDA margins reduced by 220bps y/y to 21.0%, while free cash flow also declined to a net FCF burn. To me, this contraction in profit margins amid slowing growth is a signal of a management team that isn't conservative enough or reacting quickly enough to the deteriorating macroeconomy.

Key Takeaways

In my view, Vertex is primed for a further fall as investors catch on to slowing growth. The stock is potentially set up for disappointment, as its guidance calls for slight growth acceleration across Q2-Q4 this year (though the macro is arguably worsening, and Vertex's own recent trends showcase only deceleration in growth rates). Amid a still-rich valuation that sits above peers, I'm content to monitor this stock from the sidelines and invest elsewhere for now.

data.title: U.K. Starts The Second Quarter On The Back Foot
data.description:
By James Smith

April was a disappointing month for the UK economy, judging by the 0.3% drop in GDP across the month. In truth, this isn’t hugely surprising. The figures have been enormously volatile recently. That’s partly because of frontloading activity ahead of tariffs. Manufacturing surged in February and has unwound since.

But there is also a curious trend emerging, where the GDP figures show the first half of the year – and in particular the first quarter – as stronger than the second. This has happened every year since 2022 and hints that there is an issue with the way the data is being seasonally adjusted.

Growth typically starts the year strong and gets weaker thereafter

The bottom line is that we should be taking these figures with a hefty pinch of salt. And the reality is that for some time, the Bank of England hasn’t been putting a huge amount of emphasis on this data.

That said, this week’s particularly weak jobs report hints that the risks surrounding the UK growth outlook are building. Though the figures may ultimately get revised up, they showed the biggest fall in employee numbers outside of the Covid-19 pandemic since the data began in 2014. Vacancies are falling more quickly, too.

The combination of apparent seasonality in the data, a cooling jobs market and uncertainty about the global economy suggests growth will slow materially for the remainder of the year. April’s disappointing GDP figure sets up growth of 0.1-0.2% through the second quarter, down from the eyebrow-raising 0.7% seen in the first.

All of this is a headache for Chancellor Rachel Reeves. Yesterday's spending review – an exercise which divvies out budgets across government departments – carried few surprises. Yet, it was a reminder that there's not enough money to go around and that the overall spending pot for the next few fiscal years will likely end up rising more quickly.

We think the Office for Budget Responsibility is highly likely to revise down its 2026 growth forecast at the Autumn Budget later this year, which alone would wipe out half of the Treasury's slim fiscal headroom. Further downgrades to trend productivity growth projections, as well as net migration, mean the Chancellor is likely in the red, before even considering the mounting pressures on the public purse. The overall shortfall may amount to at least £20bn, and that means tax rises are highly likely. Read more here.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Holding Steady: Evaluating fuboTV's Growth Potential Amid Market Volatility
data.description:
When I first discussed fuboTV (NYSE:FUBO) as a hold, I stated:

This stock is too risky for new investors until regulators approve the fuboTV and Hulu Live combination. After more certainty about deal approval, I will consider recommending this stock to new investors. However, after deal approval, fuboTV is only suitable for risk-tolerant, aggressive growth investors.

I also discussed how the stock price would pull back and likely be uninspiring after the initial excitement around the news of the fuboTV and Hulu Live merger died down. The stock is down 26.43% from my initial hold recommendation on January 12, 2025, to the June 4, 2025, closing price. Although it has since recovered somewhat.

Although the company is speculative and has high risk for people who want to invest today, there are reasons for growth investors who are sitting on gains to continue holding the stock. The company's first-quarter 2025 earnings report showed that it has made significant progress in improving its Adjusted EBITDA and Free Cash Flow on a trailing 12-month basis. If fuboTV continues to show improvement in its financial performance, the company could achieve profitability in 2025. Investors also remain excited about fuboTV's potential to increase its scale, subscriber base, and content offerings if tit completes the fuboTV and Hulu + Live TV merger.

This article will discuss fuboTV's aggregation model and its first-quarter 2025 earnings report. It will also examine its risks, valuation, and why I still recommend a hold.

fuboTV's super aggregation model

We've been very focused on building out a super aggregation strategy. And that strategy is very different from what other distributors are attempting to do. Again, just to be clear, everyone is focused on building an app store. We're focused on really aggregating content, leveraging technology to drive [content] discovery, and really being very user-centric and dealing with issues around fragmentation and friction in the user experience.

Source: Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") David Gandler on fuboTV's J.P. Morgan 52nd Annual Global Technology, Media and Communications Conference

The core idea of fuboTV's super-aggregation strategy is to bring content together under one roof, rather than just selling subscriptions to individual third-party apps. The idea is to get various types of content (live TV, on-demand, free ad-supported, premium, and even future pay-per-view/transactional content) together within the Fubo ecosystem and interface. This strategy should make content easier to find and contrasts with Roku's (ROKU), Amazon's (AMZN), and Alphabet's (GOOGL) (GOOG) YouTube aggregation strategy, which involves selling subscriptions to third-party platforms, such as HBO Max, Discovery+, and Paramount+, within Roku's, Amazon's, and YouTube's respective services.

fuboTV's super-aggregation strategy will utilize different payment plans for various content bundles, employing a tiered content strategy. It will also introduce a free, ad-supported tier later this year to keep users engaged during the NFL and college football offseason. The primary purpose of the free tier is to reduce customer churn during periods when viewers are watching fewer sports by providing fans with a reason to stay within the Fubo ecosystem, even if they opt out of the paid service. Over time, it's also intended to be an acquisition funnel. CEO Gandler stated at the J.P. Morgan conference:

I would say, customers that are coming in and out seasonally, and making sure we're attracting customers along the demand curve. We can't just have a $100 product. We need to have a free product and then really build on products in between so that we can dynamically move audiences throughout the year, customers throughout the year. And all of that is really supported by a really strong technology platform.

The company aims to capture a broader range of customers along the demand curve. By offering lower-priced or free options, Fubo can attract price-sensitive users who might otherwise be unwilling to subscribe. If it only had a single, high-priced offering, it would exclude a significant portion of the market that may not be able to afford the premium package. By capturing some of these customers with a free offering, it also gains the ability to upsell some of these price-sensitive consumers into lower-priced skinny bundles with programming that will keep them engaged. Remember, the company doesn't solely rely on subscriptions to generate revenue. fuboTV also generates revenue through advertising, and advertising to users on the free plan can drive revenue that would not appear on its income statement if it only had high-priced premium offerings.

Technology is the key to Fubo's "super aggregation strategy" because it enables Fubo to bring diverse content types (live TV, on-demand, free ad-supported TV ((FAST)) channels, premium add-ons, and potentially transactional video on demand/pay-per-view) into a single application, eliminating the need for users to switch between multiple apps, reducing "fragmentation and friction in the user experience."

Fubo also utilizes artificial intelligence ("AI") to facilitate easier content discovery for users. The company has created numerous innovative AI-powered features. One of these features is "Instant Headlines." The company described this feature in a press release:

Designed to help Fubo consumers make better viewing decisions in real-time, Instant Headlines surfaces current programming topics and highlights them - in 10 words or less - in the home page news carousel. The page constantly refreshes each channel tile in the carousel, updating the user on the current topic being discussed on that channel. From there, the user can click on the channel tile to watch the live segment.

The company also offers sports fans personalized game alerts, another AI-based feature that enhances user engagement by providing real-time, relevant information.

The company's first quarter of 2025 shareholder letter stated:

Fubo is the only vMVPD delivering personalized game alerts in real-time, based on past viewership and location. Key moments can include when a team has made a comeback and is within points of taking (or losing) the lead, or when a player reaches a milestone. Users can click into the game directly from the alert. These alerts are now live for NBA coverage, and we plan to roll this functionality out to other leagues. Live game scores are available on the Fubo home page to instantly update users on the status of a game and make click-through to live seamless and can be easily turned on or off to avoid spoilers, further allowing users to personally customize their streaming experience within the Fubo app.

The company also built a Playlist Service that uses AI to personalize and organize content from a user's DVRs. This service helps viewers select portions from live sporting events that they most want to watch without having to sift through hours of footage. The Playlist service analyzes recorded content to identify and organize the key moments of a game, creating custom highlight reels of specific portions. For instance, the AI-powered Playlist will assist customers in setting up recorded content playlists that show all scoring drives from the Philadelphia Eagles in the Super Bowl or show all of Stephen Curry's three-point plays in a Golden State game.

Another AI-powered feature that has gained popularity is the Teams Channel Feature, which automatically generates and organizes playlists of news clips specifically about a user's favorite teams. These news clips include game recaps and previews, analysis and opinions from pundits, interviews, and injury reports.

CEO David Gandler discussed the popularity of its Teams Channel Feature recently on the company's fourth quarter 2024 earnings call:

We continue to improve our product, we continue to add additional features. Frankly, some features--one feature in particular has really taken off, similar--it might actually have the same impact as multivu had on the industry, which is our team channels capability, which is our in-house developed AI feature that seems to have really picked up traction in the hundreds of thousands of users relatively quickly.

"Multivu" gained popularity on fuboTV several years ago. It enables users to watch multiple live events simultaneously on a single screen. Gandler sees signs of Teams Channels following in "Multivu's" footsteps.

Management made technology a core function in fuboTV's "super aggregation" strategy. fuboTV's technology offers users a personalized and engaging TV experience, differentiating the platform from other streaming services on the market.

Company fundamentals

In the first quarter, our global streaming business exceeded our subscriber forecast and achieved our revenue guidance. We are pleased with these results, which came against a typically lighter first quarter sports calendar and a broader backdrop of economic uncertainty.

CEO David Gandler on fuboTV First Quarter 2025 Earnings Call

Fubo's first-quarter 2025 North American streaming business paid subscribers decreased by 2.7% year over year to 1.47 million but exceeded the company's high point of guidance of 1.46 million. In its first quarter of 2025, North American revenue increased by 3.5% year-over-year to $407.9 million.

The company's North American Average Revenue per User (ARPU) increased by approximately 1% to $85.37. The slightly increased ARPU indicates that fuboTV was marginally successful over the last year in monetizing its existing user base through price increases, add-on packages, or increased advertising revenue per user. In a subscription-based business, increasing ARPU is a crucial driver of overall revenue growth and profitability. Management has explicitly stated its focus on achieving profitability in 2025, and rising ARPU is a vital component of this strategy, as it means the company generates more revenue from each subscriber, which can help offset increased content costs and other expenses. Ideally, investors want to see ARPU increase more robustly.

The company's Rest of World ("ROW") subscriber count decreased by 10.9% to 354,000. Its ROW revenue decreased by 0.4% year-over-year to $8.4 million. Similar to the North American business, the first quarter of 2025 ROW ARPU increased by 1% year-over-year to $7.76.

fuboTV's total subscription revenue increased by 4.74% to $391.43 million, and its total ad revenue decreased by 16.70% to $22.88 million. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") John Janedis said the following about decreasing ad revenue:

[North American ad revenue decline was] largely due to the discontinuation of Warner Bros. Discovery and TelevisaUnivision Networks. Excluding these impacts, our underlying performance improved year-over-year.

Management decided to drop content from Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and TelevisaUnivision Networks as part of the company's focus on profitability. CEO Gandler explained the decision to drop Discovery at the J.P. Morgan 52nd Annual Global Technology, Media, and Communications Conference on May 20:

So, that decision was pretty clear to us, where we can't afford to take on content like that going forward. We have a goal to reach profitability in 2025. That is our plan. And at the same time, because we do a good job really managing all of our data, do a good job really sort of managing all of our data, we have seen the fast [Free Ad-Supported Streaming Television] channels kind of step in for some of the Discovery programming to alleviate some of the losses of the channels. And I would say that at least in the first few weeks, the impact is not, let's just say it's not as great as we had anticipated. So it's more favorable, at least in the early weeks.

Its Other revenue, defined in its first quarter 10-K as "distribution fees, commissions, and carriage fees earned on sales through a channel distribution platform," increased by 69.50% to $1.973 million.

fuboTV's total revenue increased by 3.46% year-over-year to $416.266 million, beating analysts' estimates by $831,000.

Fubo's total operating expenses for the first quarter of 2025 declined 5.1% to $441.7 million. The following bar chart shows the company's global expenses as a percentage of revenue, which decreased by 10 basis points ("bps") year-over-year to 106%, indicating that the company has effectively driven costs lower and improved operational efficiency over the last year.

fuboTV's first quarter 2025 operating income improved from a $63.31 million loss in the previous year to a $25.42 million loss. Its first quarter 2025 operating margin improved from -15.73% last year to -6.11%.

fuboTV's first quarter 2025 net income from continuing operations increased from a $56.33 million loss in the previous year to a $188 million gain. Its first-quarter 2025 GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) earnings per share ("EPS") increased from a $0.19 loss in the previous period to a $0.55 gain, beating analysts' estimates by $ 0.64. CFO Janedis said on the earnings call, "It is important to note that net income includes the $220 million gain on settlement of litigation." The deal between Hulu Live and Fubo included a settlement of litigation. Fubo had previously filed a lawsuit against The Walt Disney Company (DIS), Fox (FOX), and Warner Bros. Discovery concerning a planned sports streaming platform called Venu Sports. As part of the merger agreement, Fubo settled all litigation with the defendants of the lawsuits, with Disney, Fox, and Warner Bros. Discovery making a combined payment of $220 million to Fubo.

fuboTV improved its first-quarter 2025 adjusted EPS loss to $0.02 from a loss of $0.14 a year ago. CFO Janedis commented on the earnings call, "We made meaningful progress in reducing non-operating expenses and narrowing adjusted losses."

fuboTV's first-quarter 2025 adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) was negative $1.4 million, representing a $37 million year-over-year improvement. Its first-quarter 2025 adjusted EBITDA margin improved by 930 bps to 0.3%. CFO Janesdis made the following comment on adjusted EBITDA on the earnings call, "[This improvement highlighted] our ongoing focus on cost control, efficient growth and driving leverage in the model."

fuboTV's trailing 12-month ("TTM") Cash from operations ("CFO") to Sales was 9.12%, meaning that for every $1 of sales over the last year, the company has generated $0.9. Note that the company has made significant progress in increasing the CFO that it generates from sales over the past four years.

The company's first quarter 2025 TTM CFO increased from a $163.05 million loss to a positive $152.82 million.

Its first quarter 2025 TTM non-GAAP free cash flow ("FCF") increased from a $185.34 million loss last year to a $82.15 million loss. CFO Janedis commented on the company's FCF improvement, stating, "We remain disciplined in our capital allocation and working capital management." Be aware that some websites may calculate FCF differently. For instance, Ycharts reports fuboTV's TTM FCF at a positive $133.92 million, which aligns with the calculation of another popular financial website. I suspect that Ycharts doesn't include the gain of settlement of financial litigation in its calculations, and it may use other different numbers. Fubo's FCF calculations also differ from Seeking Alpha's calculations of levered and unlevered FCF.

At the end of the first quarter, the company's cash and equivalents totaled $321.61 million, and it had $188.14 million in long-term debt, primarily in the form of convertible notes. Its debt-to-equity ratio is 0.94, meaning Fubo conservatively finances its operations with more equity than debt. Its debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 0.45, indicating that the company has a relatively small amount of debt compared to its earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. Fubo's current ratio is 0.70, which is suboptimal. This low ratio suggests that the company may struggle to fulfill its contractual obligations to media companies, sports leagues, technology partners, employees, or other short-term creditors on time, particularly if the economy deteriorates.

If the company reaches the midpoint of North American company subscriber guidance, which is 1.24 million, membership will decline 14.48% year-over-year. If it hits its North American revenue guidance of $345 million, revenue will fall 10% year over year. CFO Janedis stated the following about the company's weak second-quarter guidance: "Note that this guidance includes the continued impact of our recent drop of TelevisaUnivision content and reflects the benefit of one-time sports events in 2Q 2024." TelevisaUnivision represented a significant portion of its Hispanic content, and at least in the short term, is likely hindering Fubo's membership and revenue growth. General economic conditions likely further contributed to subscriber churn.

The outlook for the Rest of World (ROW) segment was even more concerning, with the company projecting revenue declines of 15% and subscriber drops of 17% at the midpoint of its guidance.

Suppose the company reaches the midpoint of its second-quarter 2025 North American and ROW revenue guidance. In that case, it will generate a total revenue of $352 million, falling short of analysts' second-quarter estimates before earnings of $387.68 million. After the company released its first-quarter earnings, analysts dropped their estimates for the second quarter of 2025 to $353.77 million as of the June 7 market close.

Amid an uncertain economy, the market was displeased by the potential loss of subscribers and revenue in the second quarter, and the stock dropped 17.40% on May 2, 2025.

Risks

The loss of subscribers in the first quarter, combined with the company's forecast for further losses in the second quarter, alarmed investors for several reasons. One significant issue is that fewer subscribers result in lower subscription revenue, which is Fubo's primary source of income. Its 1% ARPU increase during the first quarter was insufficient to fully offset the subscriber declines, indicating that the overall revenue pie is either shrinking or growing very slowly and is unstable. Decreasing subscribers also means fewer viewers, which can lower the volume and value of advertising inventory. Fubo emphasizes the value of its sports-first audience. However, despite advertisers' willingness to potentially pay more to reach fuboTV's sports-focused audience, its shrinking audience base makes its advertising less valuable. It makes it harder to grow overall ad revenue, especially after dropping major networks like Warner Bros. Discovery.

Another negative impact of a declining subscriber base is that Fubo loses negotiating power in content licensing negotiations. Major media companies and sports leagues prefer to license their content to platforms that can deliver a large, growing audience. CEO David Gandler explicitly mentioned on the earnings call in response to a question about fees per subscriber that "we're paying roughly 30% to 50% above market. And that doesn't include the [onerous] terms that we have in the business, which include penetration rates." Penetration rates are contractual terms that require fuboTV to include a specific channel or set of channels in a minimum percentage of subscriber packages or, more commonly, in all or most of their widely distributed packages. These terms can limit the company's ability to create skinny bundles. A decreasing subscriber base doesn't help the company reduce the fees it pays to content owners or reduce some of the onerous terms.

Decreasing subscribers not only gives the company a bad reputation among content owners but can also quickly discourage investors, who may start to believe that the company is struggling to compete, retain customers, or achieve sustainable growth. Once investors start questioning the company's ability to sustain its revenue growth, the stock price is likely to decline, making it harder for the company to raise capital and attract top talent. The potential for the company to enter a death spiral increases, where fewer subscribers result in lower revenue, which in turn leads to reduced investment in content and products, ultimately causing more subscriber losses, decreased investor interest, a declining stock price, and the death spiral repeats.

Prolonged subscriber declines could also make the Hulu Live and fuboTV merger less attractive to Disney. There is already significant bipartisan resistance in Washington to the deal. If fuboTV continues to decline in value for Disney, it may decide to back away from the fuboTV agreement to avoid the headache of navigating the numerous obstacles in Washington.

The performance of the ROW segment is worrisome. On the first quarter 2025 earnings call, Needham & Company analyst Laura Martin asked the following question that reflects the market's worries:

So David, I wanted to ask you about Rest of World. We've got the Rest of World subs down 11% for the last six months now. ARPU is down, revenues down. I know this was -- my recollection is sort of was an [indiscernible], but is this business -- is this business just in a shrink structurally? And how are you thinking about Rest of World?

Essentially, she asked the CEO about whether ROW is in a death spiral and whether Fubo still has plans for its ROW segment. Fubo's ROW operations stem from its acquisition of the French streaming provider Molotov in 2021. In response to this question about the viability of the ROW business, CEO Gandler stated:

On the Molotov side, I think we've said this many times, it's really about team technology and the synergies that we see with that business. As we've stated now since 2022, despite all of the whiplash that we've seen over this period of time from larger companies like Netflix and others and plus services, etc. Our goal has always been profitability, profitability over growth. Molotov like Fubo, we've set that same expectation, and they have to get to profitability. And we feel comfortable that they will get there. And then on the other side of that coin is the fact that we are really focused on building out our unified platform, we feel very good about where the platform is today. Fubo itself is now on that platform. We are now onboarding, Molotov and in terms of subscriber growth, it's very simple. It's a relatively straightforward formula. You have to invest in marketing. And we -- as we stated, investing in marketing is an upfront cost that has short-term cost implications, and so that has not been on the forefront for us. But yeah, international is an important piece to the long-term trajectory of this business.

Despite what appears to be the continued hemorrhaging of ROW subscribers, it seems that Fubo still has long-term plans for the business. However, the primary reason Gandler gives for the lack of subscriber growth is the desire to make the business profitable. As a result, the company has not invested aggressively in growth by increasing sales & marketing yet and is focused more on profitability, a heavy theme company-wide. So, there is a risk that ROW, which is Molotov, could enter a death spiral before becoming profitable. That risk prompted Laura Martin to ask the above question about Fubo's international business. If Molotov fails, it would be a black eye for Fubo management, and they might lose some credibility in the market and with investors. The stock price likely would not respond well to a ROW failure.

In addition to all the above risks, the economy is uncertain, and consumers are nervous. The U.S. Index of Consumer Sentiment is at 52.20, indicating that consumers lack confidence in the economy.

If the Index remains at this level or declines further, it could become significantly more challenging for Fubo to attract and retain paying subscribers and increase ARPU, threatening the company's plan to achieve profitability.

Valuation

Fubo's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is above its three- and five-year median, signaling potential overvaluation. Suppose it sold at its three-year median; its stock price would be $2.22, a 35% decline from its June 6 closing price of $3.41. If it sold at its five-year median, its stock price would be $3.13, an 8.2% drop from its June 6 closing price.

fuboTV doesn't have a similar publicly traded pure-play company. However, the following table compares its 2026 forward P/S ratio to estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) with several other publicly traded companies in the communication services sector. I am using 2026 figures instead of 2025 because analysts expect fuboTV to have negative revenue growth in 2025, rendering its 2025 P/S/G figure meaningless.

Company Name 2026 Forward P/S ratio 2026 Forward estimated revenue growth 2026 Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin (using Ycharts FCF calculations) TTM operating margin TTM ROIC (return on invested capital)
fuboTV 0.69 8.69% 0.08 8.18% -9.43 11.84%
Netflix (NFLX) 10.41 12.07% 0.86 18.52% 27.71% 24.45%
Roku (ROKU) 2.15 11.86% 0.18 7.14% -4.79 -4.33
Nexstar Media (NXST) 0.95 10.42% 0.09 21.93% 23.08% 7.33
Fox 1.64 -3.67% -0.44 14.80% 19.03% 10.00%
Warner Bros. Discovery 0.62 1.18% 0.52 11.32% 0.90% -13.93%

Except for Fox, which has an expected negative growth rate, fuboTV has the lowest one-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth, a designation it may deserve due to its higher risk. Fubo has a negative operating margin and has only recently become cash flow positive; it may struggle to pay its debt if the economy deteriorates.

Despite having a lower estimated revenue growth rate and return on invested capital (ROIC), Warner Bros. Discovery has a higher P/S/G valuation compared to fuboTV, which may be due to Warner Bros. Discovery's greater scale and ownership of significantly more intellectual property, which the market may perceive as the company having a reduced risk compared to Fubo.

On a P/S/G basis, Nexstar Media trades only slightly above Fubo despite having a better estimated 2026 revenue growth rate, a higher TTM ROIC, a higher TTM operating margin, and a higher TTM FCF. Either Nextstar deserves to sell at a higher premium to Fubo, or the market may be valuing Fubo too highly.

fuboTV's Enterprise value ("EV") to revenue is 0.716, well above its three-year median but below its five-year median, indicating that the stock has traded at lower EV/Revenue multiples, on average, in the last three years compared to its more extended history (previous five years). The chart clearly illustrates a significant drop in the EV/Revenue three-year median in 2023, which occurred due to the EV/R valuation starting to drastically tail off at the beginning of 2021. This drop likely happened due to rapid decelerating revenue growth, which quickly transitioned from triple digits (even briefly exceeding 1200%) to today's anemic single digits. Concerns about subscriber losses have also been a recent factor, prompting investors to re-rate the stock over the last several years.

The fact that it's still below its 5-year median, which makes the stock look undervalued, may be misleading because it includes peak valuation figures based on peak revenue growth from 2021 that the company is unlikely ever to achieve again. The three-year median provides a more accurate picture of the company's valuation, and that picture is one of overvaluation.

fuboTV's GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 15.77, which is below the median of 20.60 for the Communication Services sector, suggesting undervaluation. However, the company's EPS profitability is relatively recent, and the company still has negative operating margins. Therefore, it may struggle to sustain EPS profitability. When a company's earnings are volatile or barely positive, the P/E ratio can be volatile and may not accurately reflect its underlying value.

fuboTV's 2027 forward P/E-to-growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.11. Typically, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of undervaluation. Suppose the company had a PEG ratio of 1.0; its stock price would be $30.24, a 787% rise over its June 6 closing price. I used the 2027 figures because it's the first period where an investor can calculate a meaningful PEG ratio.

However, the market rarely prices in forward estimates over a year because the risks are higher that a company will fail to achieve analysts' estimates outside of a year. Additionally, the 2027 EPS projection will come off a low base, which may distort the PEG calculation. Therefore, investors should approach the PEG calculation with caution.

fuboTV is best valued using its P/S ratio or EV/R ratio, which signals overvaluation. Another reason for caution is that, although the company has a focus on profitability, if revenue growth continues to decelerate, investors may decide to re-rate its P/S and EV/R valuation lower.

I maintain a Hold rating for fuboTV

A fuboTV investment is best suited for investors with a speculative mindset. If the company can close a merger with Hulu Live, its super-aggregation strategy and focus on profitability work out, the company could potentially differentiate itself in a market where there are still 68.7 million cable subscribers who could switch to streaming services over time. As of the end of the first quarter, fuboTV only had 1.47 million North American subscribers. Therefore, the company has a long potential growth runway, even in the face of competition.

The problem is that the upside comes with significant risk. Suppose regulators succeed in blocking the fuboTV and Hulu Live deal. In that case, the company may lack the scale to attract attractive content to its platform, and the potential for a death spiral increases. There are sufficient reasons for existing investors, who are sitting on gains, to retain their shares. However, investors who have yet to buy the stock should avoid it until the company finalizes its deal with Disney, which would significantly reduce the investment risk. I maintain a hold rating for fuboTV.

Investment Philosophy

The Harbor Multi-Asset Explorer ETF (NYSEARCA:MAPP) seeks to provide long-term total return while limiting downside risk. The Fund invests primarily in underlying ETFs that provide exposure to one or more broad asset classes, such as equities, fixed income securities of any credit quality (including below-investment grade bonds, also known as high-yield bonds or “junk” bonds), real estate investment trusts, commodities, and cash and cash equivalents.

The Advisor’s broad-based approach to managing the Fund combines both systematic and discretionary research and analysis. In managing the Fund, the Advisor seeks to understand the economic and business cycle, and the risks and opportunities the current cycle presents using its proprietary multi-factor model that includes growth, liquidity, and inflation factors.

This Business Cycle Regime framework translates to a distinct asset allocation amongst Funds that are aligned to equities, fixed income, commodities, and currencies. A layer of bottom-up, fundamental research is added— potentially offering more flexibility and consideration of intangibles than traditional systematic models.

Manager Commentary

“As our base case, we expect risk assets to experience continued positive returns in the coming quarters, with economic growth remaining resilient.”

Harbor Multi-Assets Solutions Team

Market in Review

The first quarter of 2025 saw a return of volatility to financial markets, which resulted in mixed investment results across risk assets. Following the S&P 500 Index’s new all-time high midway through the quarter, those gains reversed, and the index posted its worst quarterly performance relative to the MSCI World ex-US Index in 16 years. U.S. equities experienced a sharp correction, as valuations that had become extended were coupled with investors’ new focus on the administration’s policy agenda and the resulting negative, near-term impact on economic growth and inflation.

The S&P 500 Index returned –4.3% during the quarter — its worst quarterly performance since the third quarter of 2023 — and the Russell 1000® Index returned –4.5%. Small caps continued to lag their larger peers with the Russell 2000® Index returning –9.5%. Investors’ concerns about a potential economic slowdown, in addition to uncertainty around the new administration’s economic policies, dampened consumer confidence and investor sentiment and weighed on investment returns. The quarter was marked by a reversal in market leadership within global equities, as international equities outperformed domestic equities, while U.S. Information Technology stocks, particularly the Magnificent 7, underperformed the broader equity market. Questions surrounding “American exceptionalism” and concerns about the impact of DeepSeek’s cost-efficient artificial intelligence (“AI”) model led investors to question the profitability driving the significant investment spending behind AI. A rotation away from U.S.-centric technology leadership took place during the quarter, with growth opportunities broadening internationally. Sentiment toward non-U.S. equities reached a low point, highlighted by the extreme valuation discount versus domestic equities.

Portfolio Performance

During the first quarter, the Harbor Multi-Asset Explorer ETF (“ETF”) returned 1.23% (NAV), outperforming its primary benchmark, the ICE BofA 0-3 Month US Treasury Bill Total Return Index, which returned 1.04%.

The returns from the first quarter brought the total return for the ETF to 8.19% over the trailing one-year and 13.17% since inception on September 13, 2023. The business cycle regime indicator utilized to determine the broad-based asset allocation for the ETF has remained in a late-cycle regime with market sentiment indicating a risk-seeking mentality — a regime that has historically been a positive environment for forward returns in risk assets. During the quarter, the ETF’s exposure to gold was a key driver of positive returns, as uncertainty around the new administration’s economic policies and the resulting effects on growth and inflation weighed on sentiment and attracted investors to the safe haven asset. The ETF’s exposure to international equities also helped drive positive returns during the quarter. International equities were buoyed by plans for significant fiscal packages in Europe, particularly Germany, to help rebuild their defense capabilities and infrastructure following a decade of a self-imposed “debt brake” aimed at limiting deficit spending. The ETF’s exposure to the U.S. Information Technology sector detracted from returns during the quarter. Lingering concerns surrounding the AI theme weighed on investors’ minds after the strong investment performance of many AI-exposed companies in recent quarters. Concerns about when investments in AI capabilities would deliver financial returns for companies arose as the valuation of the group was extended, and concerns about the economy began to surface.

Outlook

Harbor’s Business Cycle Regime indicator continues to signal late-cycle conditions with market sentiment remaining in risk-seeking mode at the end of the first quarter. The presidential election catalyzed an increase in consumer and business sentiment, with hopes of imminent tax cuts and deregulation driving expectations for forward growth higher. Despite the new administration’s stimulative policies, there were other factors, such as slowing immigration, unclear trade policies, and a focus on reducing government spending from elevated levels, which are acting as headwinds to economic growth. Outside the U.S., pro-growth policy changes have come out of Europe, particularly Germany, where policymakers have relaxed their self-imposed “debt brake” to allow themselves the leeway to make needed investments in areas such as defense. Following 100 basis points of interest rate cuts in 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) meeting in December acknowledged that interest rate cuts would not be a given at upcoming meetings, reflecting the uncertainty around both the effects of the policies of the new administration and the terminal rate in this cycle. Fiscal policy and tariff decisions will be key determinants on whether the Fed will deliver on the two rate cuts that have been priced into the market. Inflationary pressures within the economy have continued to decelerate, with Services (Ex-Shelter) the only subsector that is meaningfully above its pre-COVID trend. Higher tariffs pose an upside risk to inflation, and we remain focused on changes in tariff policy and their effects on growth and inflation. As our base case, we expect risk assets to experience continued positive returns in the coming quarters, with economic growth remaining resilient.

Important Information

Risks

All investments involve risk including the possible loss of principal. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. The Fund is a fund of funds and is subject to the expenses and risks of the underlying funds, including but not limited to risks associated with: commodities; derivatives; fixed income securities, including “high-yield” or “junk” bonds; foreign issuers/foreign markets, U.S. government securities; mortgage- and asset-backed securities; REITs; and small companies. The Fund’s investment performance depends upon the successful allocation by the Advisor of the Fund’s assets among asset classes, geographical regions, sectors and specific investments. The Advisor’s judgment about the attractiveness, value and growth potential of a particular asset class, region, sector or investment may be incorrect and the Advisor’s selection of the underlying funds to implement its asset allocation decisions may not produce the desired results. The Fund utilizes a quantitative model and there are limitations in every quantitative model. There can be no assurances that the strategies pursued or the techniques implemented in the quantitative model will be profitable, and various market conditions may be materially less favorable to certain strategies than others.

Benchmarks

The ICE BofA 0-3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Total Return Index measures the performance of a single issue of outstanding treasury bill which matures closest to, but not beyond, three months from the rebalancing date. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment.

The Russell 1000® Index measures the performance of the large-cap segment of the US equity securities. It is a subset of the Russell 3000 index and includes approximately 1000 of the largest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 1000® Index and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the US equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® and includes approximately 2000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 2000® Index and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

The Standard & Poor's 500 Index is an unmanaged index generally representative of the U.S. market for large capitalization equities. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

Views expressed herein may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC is the Distributor of the Harbor ETFs.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

A basis point is one hundredth of 1 percentage point.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

data.title: Buffett-Euphoria Does Not Help It - Constellation Brands Is A Sell
data.description:
Intro

Like many beverage and especially alcohol companies, Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) (NEOE:STZ:CA) has been coping with weak consumer sentiment and shifting tastes. While market indices hover around their highs, with only a few rare exceptions like Coca-Cola, most of these stocks are trading either in no-man's land or in worse cases have even seen steep declines (e.g. Pernod Ricard) – for what was considered to be a safe, predictable, low-volatility business with strong brands (moats) and high margins, where not much could go wrong.

It turned out to be different, though.

Constellation Brands falls into the category of "no-man's" land for me. But that is far from good, as I will discuss below. Its stock has been performing weak. While up by 43% (plus dividends) over the last decade, shares are sitting now almost exactly where they came from close to nine years ago.

Over the last five years and twelve months, shares are down by 5% and a painful 32% in the latter case.

Such developments don't happen out of nowhere and without a reason. While this is a call against the convinced herd, I am giving STZ a sell rating. The stock is still richly valued for what it offers. Not even the recent Buffett-hype (buying into STZ) is convincing enough for me.

Portfolio reshuffling and treading water

Part of the business strategy, not just for STZ but for the industry as such and in general, is to adjust the portfolio offerings via dispositions of out-of-favor and / or low-margin brands while occasional acquisitions are being made to replenish the pipeline. I call this "portfolio reshuffling".

The aim is obviously to flow with the consumer's taste and preferences, but it is a tough business. While ambitions and promises are high, in recent years it turned out that it's rather a treading-water operation (if not destruction of shareholder value). What I have observed through many of these companies is that growth is rather anemic while balance sheets have been ballooned as a result of dividends, buybacks and frequent deal making.

What is also not rare is that this frequent deal making comes with high hopes, high bid premiums and subsequent disappointments in the form of quiet asset write-downs, as can be seen concretely affecting Constellation Brands in the last fiscal year. STZ wrote down almost 2.8 billion USD in goodwill and 0.2 billion in intangible assets for a cool 3 billion USD, having been thrown out the window through bad deals. This is the equivalent of one year's operating cash flow. I do not like companies that are practically forced to go shopping.

If these stocks come at a premium price, as they often do, there's no reason for me to consider an investment into these equities. I need a substantial margin of safety.

Entering Constellation Brands, the stock set up for a very short-lived rally after it was announced that Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) has doubled its stake in the company.

It turned out, this was much fanfare so far, but little substance. The stock popped, and the gains evaporated quickly, like the taste of a beer that stood a few hours open. STZ shares are down more than 10% since then – while broader markets slowly moved higher.

The latest Goldman Sachs consumer staples conference was insightful. Constellation in brief highlighted that they have lowered their beer business outlook due to primarily macroeconomic headwinds and shifts in Hispanic consumer behavior. What reads like a minor and solvable aspect, is likely more than a small hiccup. Otherwise, shares would not be underperforming for such a long period.

Key macro issues obviously include (and only affect alcohol companies) rising unemployment, persistent inflation (especially compared to the pre-2020 years), low real disposable income growth (if at all) and moderated GDP growth expectations.

Regarding the second aspect, a survey highlighted concerns among Hispanic consumers about economic uncertainty and sociopolitical issues, leading to reduced spending on groceries, less frequent on-premise visits (bars / restaurants) and fewer at-home gatherings, significantly affecting beer consumption (Hispanics account for ~35% of Constellation’s beer volume, ~50% for Modelo Especial and beer is STZ's biggest segment by far).

But this is exactly what was seen as the path forward for Constellation. Thus, it is little surprising that FY26 guidance implies only and rather disappointing flat to low-single-digit volume growth. Recent shipment trends (March-May 2025) according to the conference conversation are said to be in line with expectations. During the call following the earnings release of FY25, management guided beer net sales growth to be between 0% to 3% for fiscal 2026, with an operating income growth range of 0% to 2%.

Constellation expects to maintain best-in-class 39-40% operating margins despite weaker volume growth, driven by 1-2% pricing power (below inflation) and efficiency gains. Tariffs (especially aluminum cans) are manageable due to USMCA compliance.

All in all, my impression is the company is treading water with no significant improvements. They are in the midst of selling their non-premium and weak-margin wine business, but that's just a drop in the ocean. The wine and spirits segment indeed has a significantly lower contribution to total sales vs. beer and lower operating margins, as can be seen from the last earnings release.

The transaction will modestly lower sales and slightly improve margins. Okay, but that's not enough to ignite a new fire in this story.

Multi-year guidance, Balance Sheet, and Valuation

From the same press release, but also confirmed during the Goldman conference, STZ guided for a cumulative operating cash flow of 9 billion USD over the next three years and FCF of 5 billion. The freshly started new fiscal year is expected to deliver 2.7–2.8 billion in operating cash flow and 1.5–1.6 billion USD in FCF.

Other numbers to know are a 2.2–2.4 billion USD Capex commitment with the majority of the budget flowing into the beer segment, and a new 4 billion USD buyback program (to be executed over the three-year period).

Sounds like a great plan, but it doesn't convince me.

First of all, the company currently has 12 billion USD in net debt. The coming wine-sale will result in modest proceeds, effectively lowering the debt load a bit. It won't be a big advancement. 12 billion USD in net debt against a last year's FCF of ~2 billion USD is a factor of 6x – hardly low. With the new guidance of 1.5–1.6 billion USD in FCF, the factor is much higher, even if we assume 2 billion USD in sales proceeds.

Where I likewise have my issues with is the new capital allocation plan. The dividend currently costs ~730 million USD. Assuming the buyback will be spread evenly by a third for each fiscal year (4 billion program), STZ will be repurchasing shares for ~1.3 billion USD per annum. Together with the dividend, the resulting 2 billion USD is more than the FCF protection of 1.5–1.6 billion USD.

There is modest growth guided onwards. But it needs to be delivered in the first place. Maybe the temporary issues prove to be either taking longer than expected to solve, or maybe they are even structural in nature – which I rather believe? Taking the examples of the spirits companies, it does look anything but not like a temporary challenge, even though the respective managements do not want to admit it.

Brown-Forman was the latest victim. It saw its shares crater by more than 15% after the latest earnings release. This does not look like a healthy, long-term bull market. And not like a minor one-time issue.

The business models and products aren't identical. But all these alcohol companies have in common that they have difficulties to expand, some even to hold, volumes. As price hike capabilities are limited, I see no soil for a bull market in this sector. Constellation Brands itself guided for a below-inflation price increase. They know that too much hiking will send budget-conscious consumers to value brands, which especially hurts those who focus on premium offerings like Constellation Brands.

As to the valuation, we have a market cap of 30 billion USD and as said, 12 billion USD in net debt, which limits financial flexibility as more than the entire FCF is practically tied to shareholder distributions. 42 billion USD in enterprise value against last year's 2 billion USD FCF result in an already steep 21x multiple – for very limited growth in a challenged environment. This leaves no room for error and no acceptable margin of safety. My guess is the wine sale will bring in two billion USD for a modest net debt reduction. Let's say they receive four billion USD. The new EV is then 38 billion USD. The guided FCF of 1.5–1.6 billion USD results in an even steeper forward multiple of 23–25x.

In other words, a very high premium with lots of growth already priced in. The expected FCF yield is ~4%. This is not an attractive setup. Even buying back 3% of stock back yearly does not juice this case up enough. The 2.4% dividend is no reason either to invest right now into the stock.

No matter how we look at it, due to the ongoing challenges, anemic growth, and evaporated pricing power, I see much more downside risks than upside potential (which is priced in anyhow). I would not be surprised if STZ over the next one or two earnings announcements craters, like Brown-Forman did recently. These companies have come down from astronomical valuations to still-high premium multiples – which their business environment does not reflect.

Risks to my thesis

Takeover rumors of any kind or just an announcement of a big ticket having bought a stake could send the stock higher. Also, if management executes well and finds some levers to find back to more dynamic growth, the current downtrend could end.

Conclusion

Constellation Brands, for me, is in the midst of a downtrend which I do not see ending here. The valuation is still high, while the fundamental setup does not justify such a valuation premium. With limited financial flexibility and low growth guided, STZ is a sell for me in an anyhow troubled sector.

data.title: May's Markets In Full Bloom
data.description:
In Short

  • Equity markets rebounded in May, with the S&P 500 gaining 6.2%, up 1.1% year-to-date. International markets continued an upward trend, with the All-Country World ex-U.S. index rising 14% year-to-date, benefitting from a weaker U.S. dollar.
  • “Hard” economic data has remained steady, while pockets of weakness in “soft” data raise questions. The U.S. consumer appears to be hanging on despite a period of more negative sentiment.
  • Investors should continue to adhere to long-term asset allocations. Portfolio diversification and utilization of active management where appropriate may help to mitigate downside risks during periods of volatility.

The Month in Markets

Investors may look at the calendar and be shocked to see that we are nearing the midpoint of the year, following a dizzying flurry of tariff headlines that caused sharp movements in both equity prices and bond yields. After experiencing swings that brought the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) to its highest level since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the S&P 500 is relatively unchanged year-to-date, up 1.1% through May. In fact, the index posted its best monthly performance since November 2023, rising 6.2%.

The S&P 500 equal-weighted index, with a 1.3% year-to-date return, is still narrowly outperforming its market-weighted counterpart, but the Magnificent 7 stocks came roaring back in May, gaining about 14%, with Nvidia, Microsoft and Broadcom the notable drivers of broader index performance. After being pressured by China’s DeepSeek competition earlier this year, followed by a generally risk-off environment since then, the Magnificent 7 are still down by around 3% year-to-date as a basket of stocks.1 Pockets of AI-related tailwinds, in part from the announcement of a $600 billion U.S.-Saudi Arabia AI partnership and a strong earnings season and outlook, gave technology stocks a much-needed lift. On a sector level, more defensive areas of the market such as Industrials, Utilities and Consumer Staples have fared better this year than Technology and Consumer Discretionary; this dynamic could be set to continue in any additional periods of volatility, especially once the 90-day tariff pause is over.

Despite the year’s turbulence, the U.S. economy has held relatively steady when looking at what is commonly referenced as “hard data” (i.e., any measurable, objective economic metric like GDP or employment levels). Q1 GDP showed a slight quarter-over-quarter contraction, which was primarily attributed to a surge in imports meant to get ahead of tariffs and a slight deceleration in consumer and government spending. Otherwise, the report wasn’t cause for major concern, and the second read was revised slightly higher.

April inflation data was better than expected, with CPI at its lowest year-over-year level since early 2021, following a generally disinflationary trend. The monthly employment report continued to show strength with a historically low unemployment rate and better-than-expected levels of job creation, even as continuing jobless claims hit their highest level since 2021. Domestic companies fared well in Q1, with S&P 500 earnings growing at a blended rate of about 13%—the index’s second-straight quarter of double-digit growth. Going into the second half of the year, all these indicators will be watched closely to get a sense of the impact of the administration’s policy decisions and the likely path of Federal Reserve policy (with two interest rate cuts priced in by year-end).

U.S. Wages Continue to Grow Above the Inflation Rate

To Spend or Not to Spend?

There have been some concerns this year about “soft data,” which includes surveys, sentiment indicators and expectations like consumer confidence. However, May Consumer Confidence came in at 98.0, well ahead of consensus and up from 85.7 in April. May's improvement came after five consecutive months of declines and was largely attributed to easing trade tensions after momentum in U.S.-China discussions. Since then, trade discussions seem to be in a general holding pattern, at least until July, as the U.S. works through negotiations and awaits resolution of the trade court decision disallowing tariffs. Besides trade, the Republican tax bill has been a source of both optimism on growth and worries over deficit spending.

Another cause for concern is the relative price of the U.S. dollar, which has fallen significantly so far this year, down over 8%. The pressure on the dollar has been surprising, given that higher U.S. tariffs would usually be expected to push up its relative value as U.S. consumers buy fewer foreign goods. Also, an increase in risk aversion (especially given an increase in market volatility) typically drives investors towards the haven of U.S. assets. Dollar weakness has acted as a tailwind for foreign investments, with the All-Country World ex-U.S. index up 14% year-to-date, and it is possible that downward pressure on the currency could continue.

Many of the dynamics at play bring the state of the U.S. consumer—representing about two-thirds of GDP—into question, especially as we start to look under the hood at recent spending patterns. As evident in recent earnings calls across a variety of sectors, the high-level takeaway seems to be that higher- and middle-income consumers have money to spend but are being choosier about how to spend it. This trend is shown in the strength of bellwethers like Walmart and Home Depot, with some general softness in discretionary retail, such as Target.

Restaurants, which tend to quickly reflect changes in sentiment, are showing stable spending in aggregate, but also clear winners and losers around value and innovation. Travel volume, in contrast, has slowed, particularly among airlines, as travelers postpone booking their trips. All things considered, the consumer is set up fundamentally well, with cash holdings still elevated and a supportive labor market.

As long as the consumer hangs in, we believe the potential for recession remains low. That said, the picture is mixed across sectors and industries, and we have not yet seen the full impact of tariffs or other emerging policies. Based on preliminary federal revenue data, the effective level of U.S. tariffs was about 4.5% in April and 6.5% in May, but if enacted as proposed, could eventually double or triple. We will be watching closely to see if wavering sentiment eventually translates into more meaningful action, perhaps in the form of a sustained pullback in consumer spending.

U.S. Air Travel Passenger Count

Rather than “selling in May and going away,” investors should continue to adhere to long-term strategic asset allocations. Diversification across asset class, sector, size and style, and utilizing active management where appropriate, may help mitigate downside risks during periods of volatility. This strategy has worked well so far this year; for example, a Moderate 50% stocks/50% bonds portfolio including international exposure has outperformed a domestic-only portfolio by 1.6% through May.

Portfolio Implications

Equities were higher across the board during the month, led by domestic growth stocks. We recently upgraded our views on global equities and developed non-U.S. equities due to major fiscal stimulus announced in Europe and ongoing advances in corporate governance in Japan, while maintaining an overweight to small and midcaps as we anticipate better performance against a more predictable policy backdrop and lower rates later in the year. Otherwise, we maintain an “at-target” view across equities. Admittedly, short-term volatility related to the end of the 90-day tariff pause, coupled with historical volatility in June and August due to seasonality factors, may create pressure for equities. However, any larger pullbacks (10% or more) could be an appropriate trigger to add risk, especially for those holding excess cash. In this more challenging environment, we favor employing active management to select companies with high earnings visibility.

Fixed Income was flat to mostly negative for the month after some yield volatility, although high yield moved higher as spreads tightened. We are more constructive on investment-grade fixed income as yields are close to fair value, with shorter-dated bonds in particular presenting little downside risk, in our opinion. With the spread of bond yields over cash rates likely to widen further, we see opportunities to deploy cash tactically, adding and/or shortening duration based on the movement of rates. The proposed tax bill has added a level of uncertainty to the mix, as investors may continue to demand higher term premiums to compensate for the risk of holding long-term government bonds at a time when large federal deficits are creating uncertainty about future economic stability. Multi-sector bond funds may be an appropriate vehicle to consider, given the levers a manager can opportunistically pull across sector, duration profile and region. Overall, our soft-landing outlook and debt sustainability concerns make us cautious on longer-dated bonds, even if policy rates are cut in the latter half of the year.

With a fading liquidity drought and an expected pickup in mergers and acquisitions and other deal activity due to a healthy macro backdrop, we believe significant opportunities still exist within Private Markets, as investors should expect new buyout activity and an unlocking of distributions. That said, liquidity and capital solutions providers will likely remain important to work through the substantial backlog of legacy investments. As a result, we continue to see compelling opportunities across secondaries, mid-life co-investments and capital solutions. We are cautious on core private real estate, but this is offset by what we see as abundant market-dislocation opportunities in the value-add and opportunistic sectors and, particularly, real estate secondaries. Neuberger Berman’s deep relationships and unique position within the private equity ecosystem have translated into record levels of deal flow across our platform.
